- Lapping stirrups shall be terminated with standard hooks as given in general notes, section 4.5.b with minimum lap length as specified in General Notes, section 4.6. The hooks
- NZS 3101,
- Hi Natali
- It is possible to splice the hoops at the waka, However please ensure;
- The lapping bar No 1 shall be terminated with 90Deg standard hook, as shown on the attached sketch
- The lapping bar No 2 shall be terminated with 45Deg standard hook, as shown on the attached sketch
- The lapping bars No 1 and No 2 shall have minimum splice length of 190mm,
- Please make sure the hooks to be anchored around longitudinal bars

_Captured Sunday, 7 January 2018 5:17 p.m. · General › Concrete Design_

**Diagrams**
![deflection](https://sattarfd.github.io/image-host/gen_464_deflection_1.png)

- Design Aid;
- Beam Deflection Formula
- Hi Kwan
- I can’t comment on the relationship between strength and elastic modulus, or the appropriate transfer time, but could the following material-related factors have contributed:
- Was the compressive strength measured on match-cured cylinders?
- Was the concrete shortening significantly as a result of ongoing cooling or drying after transfer?
- Were the deflections greater than those observed on previous castings from similar concrete? If so, has the concrete supplier changed materials, eg higher cement content (to achieve 35MPa as soon as possible in cold weather) or different coarse aggregate source/size/content, or different mix design?
- Was air content correct?
- Was the load applied slower than usual?
- NZS 3101 5.2.3(c) says to use f’c +10
- Perhaps the Bridge Manual is more informative?
- Also ask Dave Idle or Dejan, or one of the bridge team in Chch – someone will have encountered this before.
- Regards
- 510..2 Sec14.2
- Since the solid fence is not for human oc

- Shrinkage
- plastic shrinkage, chemical shrinkage, thermal shrinkage and drying shrinkage
- Plastic shrinkage
- wet concrete
- chemical
- thermal
- drying
- hardened concrete
- Chemical shrinkage; occurs rapidly in the days and weeks after casting and is less dependent on the environment and the size of the specimen than drying shrinkage
- Thermal shrinkage; is the contraction that results in the first few hours (or days) after setting.
- endogenous shrinkage
- Drying and autogenous shrinkage both increase with time at a decreasing rate.
- Drying shrinkage may continue for many years depending on the size and shape of the specimen, but autogenous shrinkage is essentially complete at about 50 da

- http://www.concrete.org.uk/fingertips-nuggets.asp?cmd=display&id=903
- Fixings that are self-tapping or self-drilling without a plug may be suitable.
- Expandable fixings where a nylon plug is inserted into a hole and a pin driven or screwed into the plug can take slightly heavier loads.
- Other methods include flexible pins that are driven straight into the no-fines that deflect around the aggregate lodging in the void and expanding foam adhesives.
- Accurate location of fixings may not be possible. The use of brackets or battens securely located into the no-fines may be necessary onto which objects can then be fixed with more precision.

**Following loss may be included in the loss**
- Extension of strand
- Extension of bolts
- Shortening of strut
- Initial elastic shortening of the unit
- Curing temperature effect
- Wedge draw in

**Temperature Curing**
- It is assumed that at time of casting both concrete and strands are hot (40
- °
- C).
- Due to temperature curing, both remain hot till bond between the strands and concrete had happen
- Then the beams are get cool to service temperature, concrete hold back the strands so reduces the prestress force.

- INSTALLATION GUIDE;
- ReidBar™-Steel-Components-Specification-Installation-Guide-Electronic-July-2016
- HI Sattar,
- Thank you for your email.
- There are no stressing requirements for the performance of
- ReidBar™ components.
- Provided the bar is screwed fully through the component, the full breaking strength of the bar will be developed.
- Reid™ recommends using a wrench with a minimum length of 300mm to ensure the bar is fully engaged.
- The size of the recess shown on the drawing looks adequate to tight the elements together unless there are Specific Design stressing requirements. It that's the case, please let us know we will provide you guidance on that.
- Please refer to the extract below from previously attached documents.
- Have a great weekend.

- Reference documents;
- ACI 224.4R-13 - Guide to Design Detailing to Mitigate Cracking
- ACI 360R-10 Guide to Design of Slabs-on-Ground
- Slabs-on-ground are considered non-structural elements where applied loads are passed directly though the slab to the ground.
- Cracking in non-structural slabs-on-ground can be mitigated and controlled to a significant extent by the proper use of contraction, construction, expansion, and isolation joints.
- Other approaches to mitigating or controlling cracks, such as expansive cement concrete, post-tensioning, or steel and synthetic fiber reinforcement, are not covered in this chapter, but are covered in ACI 360R.
- Other factors can influence cracking, such as subgrade support, friction between slab and subgrade, reinforcement quantity and details, concrete materials, mixture proportions, and curing and ambient weather during construction.
- Application time;
- It is desirable to install contraction joints early, before volume changes of the concrete occur due t

- Has chemical analysis of the water been carried out to determine the softness of the water?
- This may affect the exposure class for the elements in contact with running water.
- Chemical analysis has not been undertaken.
- Table 3.1 of NZS 3101 assumes that members are in constant contact with water whereas Table 8 of NZS 3109 provides further information and differentiates between predominantly submerged and alternating wet and dry conditions. For alternating wet and dry conditions, NZS 3109 classifies the exposure as either B1 or B2 regardless of water hardness. Classification U is only applied to the flowing, predominantly submerged condition.
- The column bases are above the low water level and normal water levels fluctuate throughout the day based on outflows from the upstream dams. As such, the columns are considered to be in the alternating wet and dry condition and therefore exposure classification B
